subject: Mathematics
q: The surface area of a balloon of spherical shape being inflated, increases at a constant rate. If initially, the radius of balloon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> units and after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> seconds, it becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> units, then its radius after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> seconds is
question: The surface area of a balloon of spherical shape being inflated, increases at a constant rate. If initially, the radius of balloon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> units and after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> seconds, it becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> units, then its radius after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> seconds is
solution:
Let surface area of the spherical balloon A=4πr2

dAdt=8πrdrdt=k (let)    ...1

On integrating on both sides w.r.t t , we get

4πr2=kt+C . 

Given that, at t=0, r=3.

⇒36π=C

Also given that, at t=5, r=7

⇒4π×49=5k+36π

⇒5k=4π49-9   ⇒5k=4π×40 

⇒k=32π

On substituting k value in equation 1we get, 4πr2=32πt+36π

⇒r2=8t+9

Given t=9.

⇒r2=81⇒r=9.

q: The order and degree of the differential equation $\left(1+3 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2 / 3}=4 \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}$ are
question: The order and degree of the differential equation $\left(1+3 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2 / 3}=4 \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}$ are
solution: Order = 3, degree = 3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(x)=x-1$ and $g(x)=e^x$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$. If $\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(e^{-2 \sqrt{x}} g(f(f(x)))-\frac{y}{\sqrt{x}}\right), y(0)=0$, then $y(1)$ is :-
question: Let $f(x)=x-1$ and $g(x)=e^x$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$. If $\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(e^{-2 \sqrt{x}} g(f(f(x)))-\frac{y}{\sqrt{x}}\right), y(0)=0$, then $y(1)$ is :-
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x-1 \\ & f(f(x))=f(x)-1=x-1-1=x-2 \\ & g(f(f(x)))=e^{x-2} \\ & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{-2 \sqrt{x}} \times e^{x-2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} y \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} y=e^{x-2 \sqrt{x}-2} \text { which is L.D.E } \\ & \text { I.F. }=e^{\int \frac{d y}{\sqrt{x}}}=e^{2 \sqrt{x}}
\end{aligned}$
Its solution is
$\begin{aligned}
& y \times e^{2 \sqrt{x}}=\int e^{2 \sqrt{x}} \times e^{x-2 \sqrt{x}-2} d x+c \\ & y \times e^{2 \sqrt{x}}=\int e^{x-2} d x+c \\ & y \times e^{2 \sqrt{x}}=e^{x-2}+c
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Given } \mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{y}=0 \Rightarrow 0=\mathrm{e}^{-2}+\mathrm{c} \quad ; \mathrm{c}=-\mathrm{e}^{-2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{y} \times \mathrm{e}^{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}-2}-\mathrm{e}^{-2}$
$\text { when } \mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y} \times \mathrm{e}^2=\mathrm{e}^{-1}-\mathrm{e}^{-2}$
$y=\frac{e^{-1}-e^{-2}}{e^2}=\frac{\frac{1}{e}-\frac{1}{e^2}}{e^2}=\frac{e^2-e}{e^5}=\frac{e-1}{e^4}$
Option (1) is correct

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sec</mi><mi>x</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>tan</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sec</mi><mi>x</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>tan</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given: secxdy+21-xtanx+x2-xdx=0

⇒secxdy=2x-1tanx+x2-2xdx

⇒dycosx=2x-1sinxcosx+x2-2xdx

⇒dy=2x-1sinx+x2-2xcosxdx

⇒∫dy=∫2x-1sinx+x2-2xcosxdx

⇒∫dy=∫2x-1sinx+∫x2-2xcosxdx

⇒y(x)=∫2(x-1)sinxdx+x2-2x(sinx)-∫(2x-2)sinxdx

⇒y(x)=x2-2xsinx+λ

It is given that, y0=2

⇒y(0)=0-0sin0+λ

⇒2=0+λ

⇒λ=2

⇒y(x)=x2-2xsinx+2

⇒y(2)=22-2×2sin2+2

⇒y2=2

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is the solution curve of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mi>tan</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>sec</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is the solution curve of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mi>tan</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>sec</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given:

dydx+ytanx=xsecx

This is a linear differential equation.

I.F.=e∫tanxdx=secx

Then solution of differential equation is

ysecx=∫xsec2xdx

⇒ysecx=xtanx-∫tanxdx

⇒ysecx=xtanx-lnsecx+C

Given:

y0=1⇒c=1

∴ysecx=xtanx-lnsecx+1

At x=π6, we get

ysecπ6=π6tanπ6-lnsecπ6+1

⇒y23=π613-ln23+1

⇒y=π12-32ln23+32

⇒y=π12-32ln23-lne

⇒y=π12-32loge2e3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>f</mi></math> be a polynomial function such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mi>′</mi></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><msup><mi>f</mi><mrow><mi>′</mi><mi>′</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> for all <math><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then :
question: Let <math><mi>f</mi></math> be a polynomial function such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mi>′</mi></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><msup><mi>f</mi><mrow><mi>′</mi><mi>′</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> for all <math><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then :
solution:
Degree of fx will be 3

f(x)=ax3+bx2+cx+d

f3x=27ax3+9bx2+3cx+d

f'(x)=3ax2+2bx+c

f′′x=6ax+2b

f3x=f′x f′′x

Comparing the coefficient, we get

27a=18a2 ⇒a= 3 2

Also b=0, c=0, d=0

fx=32x3, f( 2 )=12

⇒ f′x=92x2, f′′x=9x

Hence, f'(2)=18, f''(2)=18

Hence, f''2−f'(2)=0

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
We have,

2+sinxdydx=-y+1cosx

⇒∫dyy+1=-∫cosxsinx+2dx

Let, sinx=t⇒cosxdx=dt

⇒logy+1=-∫dtt+2+C

where, C is the constant of integration.

⇒logy+1+logsinx+2-C=0

⇒logy+1sinx+2=C

Given, y0=1

⇒C=log4.

⇒logy+1sinx+2=log4    .....i

Now, put x=π2 in the equation i, we get, logy+1+log3-log4=0

⇒ logy+1=log43

⇒y+1=43

⇒y=43-1=13.

subject: Mathematics
q: The differential equation of the family of circles passing through the origin and having centre at the line $y=x$ is :
question: The differential equation of the family of circles passing through the origin and having centre at the line $y=x$ is :
options: $\left(x^2-y^2+2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left(x^2-y^2-2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} y$
$\left(x^2+y^2+2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left(x^2+y^2-2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} y$
$\left(x^2+y^2-2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left(x^2+y^2+2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} y$
$\left(x^2-y^2+2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left(x^2-y^2+2 x y\right) \mathrm{d} y$
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& C \equiv x^2+y^2+g x+g y=0....(1)\\
& 2 x+2 y y^{\prime}+g+g y^{\prime}=0 \\
& g=-\left(\frac{2 x+2 y y^{\prime}}{1+y^{\prime}}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Put in (1)
$\begin{aligned}
& x^2+y^2-\left(\frac{2 x+2 y y^{\prime}}{1+y^{\prime}}\right)(x+y)=0 \\
& \left(x^2-y^2-2 x y\right) y^{\prime}=x^2-y^2+2 x y
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be two distinct solutions of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> respectively. Then, the number of points of intersection of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be two distinct solutions of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> respectively. Then, the number of points of intersection of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given,

dydx=x+y⇒dydx-y=x

So, IF=e∫-1dx=e-x

Now solution is given by ye-x=∫xe-xdx

⇒ye-x=-xe-x-e-x+c

⇒y=-x-1+cex

Given, y10=0⇒c=1

∴ y1=-x-1+ex      ⋯1

Also given y20=1⇒c=2

∴ y2=-x-1+2ex     ⋯2

Now from equation 1 & 2 we get,  y2-y1=ex>0    ∴  y2≠y1

∴  Number of points of intersection of y1 and y2 is zero.

subject: Mathematics
q: The differential equation representing the family of ellipses having foci either on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis or on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis, center at the origin and passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The differential equation representing the family of ellipses having foci either on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis or on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis, center at the origin and passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Let the equation of ellipse be x2a2+y2b2=1

It passes through 0,3⇒x2a2+y29=1  ...i 

Differentiating w.r.t. x,

⇒2xa2+2y9dydx=0

1a2=-y9xy'  ...ii

From i and ii we get,

-xy9y'+y29=1

⇒xyy'-y2+9=0

subject: Mathematics
q: The solution of the differential equation, $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x-y)^{2}$, when $y(1)=1,$ is:
question: The solution of the differential equation, $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=(x-y)^{2}$, when $y(1)=1,$ is:
solution:
The given differential equation

$\frac{d y}{d x}=(x-y)^{2}$

Let $x-y=t \Rightarrow 1-\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{d t}{d x}$

Now, from equation (1)

$\left(1-\frac{d t}{d x}\right)=(t)^{2}$

$\Rightarrow \quad 1-t^{2}=\frac{d t}{d x} \Rightarrow \int d x=\int \frac{d t}{1-t^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow-x=\frac{1}{2 \times 1} \ln \left|\frac{t-1}{t+1}\right|+c$

$\Rightarrow-x=\frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{x-y-1}{x-y+1}\right|+c$

$\because$ The given condition $y(1)=1$

$-1=\frac{1}{2} \ln \left|\frac{1-1-1}{1-1+1}\right|+c \Rightarrow c=-1$

Hence, $2(x-1)=-\ln \left|\frac{1-x+y}{1-y+x}\right|$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the general solution of the differential equation $y^{\prime}=\frac{y}{x}+\Phi\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)$, for some function $\Phi$, is given by $y \ln |c x|=x$, where $\mathrm{c}$ is an arbitrary constant, then $\Phi(2)$ is equal to:
question: If the general solution of the differential equation $y^{\prime}=\frac{y}{x}+\Phi\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)$, for some function $\Phi$, is given by $y \ln |c x|=x$, where $\mathrm{c}$ is an arbitrary constant, then $\Phi(2)$ is equal to:
solution:
Given $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\phi\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$
Let $\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=v$ so that $y=x v$ or $\frac{d y}{d x}=x \frac{d v}{d x}+v$
from (1) \& (2), $x \frac{d v}{d x}+v=v+\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)$ or, $\frac{d v}{\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)}=\frac{d x}{x}$
Integrating both sides, we get $\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \frac{d v}{\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)} \Rightarrow \ln x+c=\int \frac{d v}{\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)}$
(where $\mathrm{c}$ being constant of integration)
But, given $\mathrm{y}=\frac{x}{\ln |c x|}$ is the general solution
so that $\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{v}=\ln |c x|=\int \frac{d v}{\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)}$
Differentiating w.r.t $v$ both sides, we get $\phi\left(\frac{1}{v}\right)=\frac{-1}{v^2} \Rightarrow \phi\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=-\frac{y^2}{x^2}$
when $\frac{x}{y}=2$ i.e. $\phi(2)$
$$
=-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2=-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{-1}{4}\right)
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>;</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to ___.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>;</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to ___.
solution:
We have, ∫e-ydy=∫eαxdx

⇒-e-y=eαxα+c      ...1 

Put x,y=ℓn2,ℓn2

-12=2αα+c       ...2

Put x,y≡0,-ℓn2 

-2=1α+c

From equation 1-2, we get

2α-1α=32

⇒α=2( as α∈N).

subject: Mathematics
q: If the curve $y = f(x)$ passes through the point $(1, e)$ and satisfies the differential equation $dy = y(2 + \log_e x)\,dx$, $x > 0$, then $f(e)$ is equal to :
question: If the curve $y = f(x)$ passes through the point $(1, e)$ and satisfies the differential equation $dy = y(2 + \log_e x)\,dx$, $x > 0$, then $f(e)$ is equal to :
solution:
Given differential equation is $\dfrac{dy}{y} = (2 + \log_e x) dx$

Integrating both sides, we get:

$\int \dfrac{dy}{y} = \int (2 + \log_e x) dx$

$\log_e y = 2x + x \log_e x - x + C$

$\log_e y = x + x \log_e x + C$

Since the curve passes through $(1, e)$, substitute $x = 1$ and $y = e$:

$\log_e e = 1 + 1 \cdot \log_e 1 + C$

$1 = 1 + 0 + C \Rightarrow C = 0$

The equation of the curve is $\log_e y = x + x \log_e x$

To find $f(e)$, substitute $x = e$:

$\log_e y = e + e \log_e e$

$\log_e y = e + e(1) = 2e$

$y = e^{2e}$

Answer: $e^{2e}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given

dydx+exx2−2y=x2−2xx2−2e2x

It is linear differential equation so,

IF=e∫x2-2exdx

=ex2ex-2∫xexdx-2ex  =ex2ex-2xex-ex-2ex

IF=ex2-2xex

Now solution is given by

y×ex2-2xex=∫ex2-2xex×x2-2xx2-2e2x

=∫ex2-2xex×x2-2xexx2-2ex

Now let  x2-2xex=t

We get exx2-2x+ex2x-2dx=dt

exx2-2x+2x-2dx=dt 

exx2-2dx=dt

y×ex2-2xex=∫et×t×dt

y×ex2-2xex=ett-1+c

y×ex2-2xex=ex2-2xexx2-2xex-1+c

Now at x=0  y=0

0×e0=e00xex-1+c

0=-1+c  c=1

Putting the value of c=1 we get the equation as

⇒y×ex2-2xex=ex2-2xexx2-2xex-1+1

Now putting x=2 in equation we get

y×e4-4e2=e4-4e24-4e2-1+1

⇒y×e0=e00e2-1+1

⇒y=-1+1=0

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, dydx+2x-y2y-12x-1=0  x, y>0, y1=1

Now rearranging and integrating both side of  dydx=-2x2y-12y2x-1, we get

⇒∫2y2y-1dy=-∫2x2x-1dx

⇒1ln2∫2yln22y-1dy=-1ln2∫2xln22x-1dx

⇒1ln2ln2y-1=-1ln2ln2x-1+C

At x=1,y=1

Putting this values in above equation we get C=0

So, ln2y-1+ln2x-1=0

⇒2x-12y-1=1

⇒2y-1=12x+1

At x=2

⇒2y=13+1=43

y=log243=log24-log23=2-log23

subject: Mathematics
q: The solution curve of the differential equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> which passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is
question: The solution curve of the differential equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> which passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is
solution:
1+e-x1+y2dydx=y2

Separate variables

⇒1+y2dyy2=dx1+e-x

Integrate both side

⇒∫1+y2dyy2=∫dx1+e-x

⇒∫1+1y2dy=∫exdxex+1

⇒y-1y=lnex+1+C

Since curve pass through 0, 1.

⇒1-11=lne0+1+C ⇒C=-ln2

⇒y-1y=lnex+1-ln2

⇒y2-1y=lnex+12

⇒y2=1+yloge1+ex2

q: If y=yx is the solution of the differential equation dydx+tanxy=sinx,0≤x≤π3, with y0=0, then yπ4 is equal to
question: If y=yx is the solution of the differential equation dydx+tanxy=sinx,0≤x≤π3, with y0=0, then yπ4 is equal to
solution:
dydx+tanxy=sinx; 0≤x≤π3

I.F.=e∫tanxdx=elnsecx=secx

ysecx=∫tanxdx+C

ysecx=lnsecx+C

x=0, y=0  ⇒ C=0

ysecx=lnsecx

y=cosx·lnsecx

yx=π4=12·ln2

yx=π4=122loge2

subject: Mathematics
q: If $x \frac{d y}{d x}=y(\log y-\log x+1)$, then the solution of the equation is
question: If $x \frac{d y}{d x}=y(\log y-\log x+1)$, then the solution of the equation is
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{x d y}{d x}=y(\log y-\log x+1) \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}\left(\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Put $y=v x$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=v+\frac{x d v}{d x}$
$\Rightarrow v+\frac{x d v}{d x}=v(\log v+1)$
$\frac{x d v}{d x}=v \log v$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{v \log v}=\frac{d x}{x}$
put $\log v=z$
$\frac{1}{v} d v=d z$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{z}=\frac{d x}{x}$
$\ln z=\ln x+\ln c$
$z=c x$
$\log v=c x$
$\log \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c x$

subject: Mathematics
q: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> (where C is a constant of integration)
question: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> (where C is a constant of integration)
solution:
1+x21+y2+xydydx=0

Integrating,

⇒∫2y21+y2dy=-∫1+x2x1+x2dx
⇒1+y2=-∫x1+x2dx-∫xx21+x2dx

Put 1+x2=t⇒x2=t2-1 to solve RHS 2nd integration, 

⇒1+y2=-1+x2-∫tt2-1tdt
⇒1+y2+1+x2=-∫1t2-1dt
⇒1+y2+1+x2=12ln1+x2+11+x2-1+C

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\left(x^2+4\right)^2 d y+\left(2 x^3 y+8 x y-2\right) d x=0$. If $y(0)=0$, then $y(2)$ is equal to
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\left(x^2+4\right)^2 d y+\left(2 x^3 y+8 x y-2\right) d x=0$. If $y(0)=0$, then $y(2)$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}+y\left(\frac{2 x^3+8 x}{\left(x^2+4\right)^2}\right)=\frac{2}{\left(x^2+4\right)^2} \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}+y\left(\frac{2 x}{x^2+4}\right)=\frac{2}{\left(x^2+4\right)^2} \\
& \text { IF }=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^2+4} d x} \\
& \text { IF }=x^2+4 \\
& y \times\left(x^2+4\right)=\int \frac{2}{\left(x^2+4\right)^2} \times\left(x^2+4\right) \\
& y\left(x^2+4\right)=2 \int \frac{d x}{x^2+2^2} \\
& y\left(x^2+4\right)=\frac{2}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+c \\
& 0=0+c=c=0 \\
& y\left(x^2+4\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \\
& y \text { at } x=2 \\
& y(4+4)=\tan ^{-1}(1) \\
& y(2)=\frac{\pi}{32}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $y(x)$ is the solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x+1}{x}\right) y=e^{-2 x}, x>0,$ where $y(1)=\frac{1}{2} e^{-2},$ then:
question: If $y(x)$ is the solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x+1}{x}\right) y=e^{-2 x}, x>0,$ where $y(1)=\frac{1}{2} e^{-2},$ then:
solution:
Given differential equation is,

$\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(2+\frac{1}{x}\right) y=e^{-2 x}, x>0$

$\mathrm{IF}=e^{\int\left(2+\frac{1}{x}\right) d x}=e^{2 x+\ln x}=x e^{2 x}$

Complete solution is given by

$y(x) \cdot x e^{2 x}=\int x e^{2 x} \cdot e^{-2 x} d x+c$

$=\int x d x+c$

$y(x) \cdot e^{2 x} \cdot x=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+c$

Given, $y(1)=\frac{1}{2} e^{-2}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} e^{-2} \cdot e^{2} \cdot 1=\frac{1}{2}+c \Rightarrow c=0$

$\therefore \quad y(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{-2 x}}{x}$

$y(x)=\frac{x}{2} \cdot e^{-2 x}$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$

$y^{\prime}(x)=\frac{e^{-2 x}}{2}(1-2 x)  < 0 \forall x \in\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)$

Hence, $y(x)$ is decreasing in $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)$

subject: Mathematics
q: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
question: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
solution:
Given, x-y2dx+y5x+y2dy=0

After rearranging we get,

dydx=y2-xy5x+y2 

Now, let y2=v⇒2ydydx=dvdx

12ydvdx=v-xy5x+v

dvdx=2v-x5x+v

Now let v=kx

⇒k+xdkdx=2kx-x5x+kx

⇒xdkdx=-k2+3k+2k+5

⇒∫5+kk+1k+2dk=∫-dxx

⇒∫4k+1-3k+2dk=-∫dxx

⇒4lnk+1-3lnk+2=-lnx+lnc

⇒k+14k+23=cx

⇒vx+14vx+23=cx

⇒cy2+2x3=y2+x4

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x\sqrt{1-x^2}\,dy + \left(y\sqrt{1-x^2} - x\cos^{-1}x\right)dx = 0$, $x \in (0, 1)$, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^-} y(x) = 1$. Then $y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$ equals:
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x\sqrt{1-x^2}\,dy + \left(y\sqrt{1-x^2} - x\cos^{-1}x\right)dx = 0$, $x \in (0, 1)$, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^-} y(x) = 1$. Then $y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$ equals:
solution:
The given differential equation is:

$x\sqrt{1-x^2}\,dy + \left(y\sqrt{1-x^2} - x\cos^{-1}x\right)dx = 0$

Rearranging the terms, we get:

$x\sqrt{1-x^2}\,dy + y\sqrt{1-x^2}\,dx = x\cos^{-1}x\,dx$

Dividing the entire equation by $\sqrt{1-x^2}$:

$x\,dy + y\,dx = \dfrac{x\cos^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$

The left side is the exact differential of $xy$:

$d(xy) = \dfrac{x\cos^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$

Integrating both sides:

$xy = \int \dfrac{x\cos^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx + C$

To evaluate the integral, use integration by parts. Let $u = \cos^{-1}x$ and $dv = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$.

$du = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$ and $v = -\sqrt{1-x^2}$

$\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$

$\int \dfrac{x\cos^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx = -\sqrt{1-x^2}\cos^{-1}x - \int \left(-\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)\left(-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)\,dx$

$= -\sqrt{1-x^2}\cos^{-1}x - \int 1\,dx$

$= -\sqrt{1-x^2}\cos^{-1}x - x$

Substituting this back into the equation:

$xy = -\sqrt{1-x^2}\cos^{-1}x - x + C$

It is given that $\lim_{x\to 1^-} y(x) = 1$. Substituting $x = 1$ and $y = 1$:

$1(1) = -\sqrt{1-1^2}\cos^{-1}(1) - 1 + C$

$1 = 0 - 1 + C \Rightarrow C = 2$

The particular solution is:

$xy = 2 - x - \sqrt{1-x^2}\cos^{-1}x$

To find $y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$, substitute $x = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$\dfrac{1}{2}y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = 2 - \dfrac{1}{2} - \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2}\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{2}y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{3}{2} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{2}y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{3}{2} - \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}$

Multiplying by $2$:

$y\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = 3 - \dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The difference between degree and order of a differential equation that represents the family of curves given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msqrt><mi>a</mi></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> is _______.
question: The difference between degree and order of a differential equation that represents the family of curves given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msqrt><mi>a</mi></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> is _______.
solution:
y2=ax+a2=ax+a3/22   ...1

⇒2yy'=a

Put in equation 1 we get, 

y2=2yy'x+2yy'3/22

y2-2xyy'=2yy'3/22

Squaring both the sides we get, 

y2-2xyy'2=2y3y'3

∴ Order =1

Degree =3

Degree - order =3-1=2

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}$, $y(1)=0$. Then $y(0)$ is
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}$, $y(1)=0$. Then $y(0)$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{1+x^2}=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{1+x^2} \\ & \text { I.F. }=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+x^2} d x}=e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \\ & y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}=\int\left(\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{1+x^2}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \cdot d x \\ & \text { Let } \tan ^{-1} x=z \quad \therefore \frac{d x}{1+x^2}=d z \\ & \therefore y \cdot e^z=\int e^{2 z} d z=\frac{e^{2 z}}{2}+C \\ & y \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} x}=\frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} x}}{2}+C \\ & \Rightarrow y=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}{2}+\frac{C}{e^{\tan ^{-1} x}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \because \mathrm{y}(1)=0 \Rightarrow 0=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}}{2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}}{2 \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{y}(0)=\frac{1-\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement 1: The degrees of the differential equations $\frac{d y}{d x}+y^2=x$ and $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y=\sin x$ are equal.
Statement 2: The degree of a differential equation, when it is a polynomial equation in derivatives, is the highest positive integral power of the highest order derivative involved in the differential equation, otherwise degree is not defined.
question:
Statement 1: The degrees of the differential equations $\frac{d y}{d x}+y^2=x$ and $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y=\sin x$ are equal.
Statement 2: The degree of a differential equation, when it is a polynomial equation in derivatives, is the highest positive integral power of the highest order derivative involved in the differential equation, otherwise degree is not defined.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true; Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
Statement-1
Given differential equations are $\frac{d y}{d x}+y^2=x$ and $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y=\sin x$
Their degrees are 1 .
Both have equal degree.
Also, Statement $-2$ is the correct explanation for Statement - 1.

subject: Mathematics
q: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is (where c is a constant of integration)
question: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is (where c is a constant of integration)
solution:
Given differential equation can be written as:
y2 dx-xy dy=x3 dx
⇒y dx-x dy=x3y dx
⇒ y dx-x dyy2=xy3 dx
⇒ ∫dxyxy3= ∫dx
⇒ xy-3+1 -3+1=x+c

⇒ y2=-2x3-cx2

⇒y2+2x3+cx2=0

subject: Mathematics
q: If the solution curve of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> then the abscissa of the point on the curve whose ordinate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced></math> is
question: If the solution curve of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> then the abscissa of the point on the curve whose ordinate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced></math> is
solution:
tan-1y-xdy=1+y2dx

⇒dxdy+11+y2x=tan-1y1+y2

I.F. =e∫11+y2dy=etan-1y

General solution will be

xetan-1y=∫etan-1y×tan-1y1+y2dy

⇒xetan-1y=tan-1yetan-1y-etan-1y+C

Since the curve passes through 1,0  ⇒  C=2

So, the curve reduces to xetan-1y=tan-1yetan-1y-etan-1y+2

when y=tan1, xe=1e-e+2

⇒  x=2e

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} y=x \mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)}} ; y(0)=0 .$
Then the area enclosed by the curve $f(x)=y(x) \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)}}$ and the line $y-x=4$ is__________
question:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} y=x \mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)}} ; y(0)=0 .$
Then the area enclosed by the curve $f(x)=y(x) \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)}}$ and the line $y-x=4$ is__________
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & I F=e^{\int \frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} d x}=e^{\frac{-1}{1+x^2}} \\ & y \cdot e^{\frac{-1}{1+x^2}}=\int x \cdot e^{\frac{1}{1+x^2}} \cdot e^{\frac{-1}{1+x^2} d x} \\ & y \cdot e^{\frac{-1}{1+x^2}}=\frac{x^2}{2}+c \\ & (0,0) \Rightarrow C=0 \\ & y(x)=\frac{x^2}{2} e^{\frac{1}{1+x^2}} \\ & f(x)=\frac{x^2}{2}\end{aligned}$
$A=\int_{-2}^4(x+4)-\frac{x^2}{2} d x=18$
